hero.subheadline: macOS Updates keeps system updates, App Store apps, Homebrew, native CLI tools, npm globals, and 40+ internet apps current while touching only software already installed on this Mac.

problemSolution.title: Stop maintaining your Mac one updater at a time.
problemSolution.subtitle: macOS has system updates, App Store updates, Homebrew, npm globals, vendor auto-updaters, DMGs, and GUI-only iPad apps. macOS Updates turns that sprawl into one auditable run.
problemSolution.withDesc: Inventory first. Update only what is present. Record what changed.

whyUpdate.title: Your local inventory is the source of truth.
whyUpdate.subtitle: The installer scans this Mac, builds its own APPLICATIONS.md, and never imports another user's app list or installs missing apps from a maintainer catalog.

stats.timeBefore: Typical manual pass through System Settings, App Store, brew, npm globals, and vendor update dialogs.
stats.timeAfter: One dry-run can preview the full workflow without applying mutations.
stats.sources: macOS, App Store, native CLI/npm, Homebrew, internet apps, inventory, and postupdate history.

security.title: Correct privileged calls
security.desc: macOS updates run with softwareupdate -ia -R, and App Store updates use sudo mas upgrade for current macOS behavior.

compatibility.title: Installed-only policy
compatibility.desc: Supported-but-missing apps are reported, not installed. Unknown installed apps become coverage work.

automation.title: Private overlay ready
automation.desc: Public scripts stay in GitHub while APPLICATIONS.md, UPDATES.md, preferences, and secrets can live in dev_sync.

whyUpdate.callout: "The difference is the inventory. macOS Updates starts from what is actually on the machine, then chooses the right updater for each layer."

pipeline.title: One orchestrator, seven update layers.
pipeline.subtitle: update_all.sh runs prescan, macOS, App Store, native CLI/npm, Homebrew, internet apps, and postupdate bookkeeping in order.

check.name: Prescan
check.desc: Scan /Applications, Homebrew, and App Store receipts into this Mac's APPLICATIONS.md.
check.detail: Public installs never import another user's inventory.

plan.name: System + Store
plan.desc: Apply macOS updates with reboot metadata, then run the two-track App Store updater.
plan.detail: softwareupdate keeps -R; mas runs through sudo; iPad apps use GUI automation when needed.

apply.name: CLI + Brew
apply.desc: Update native Node/Bun, npm global CLIs, Homebrew formulae, casks, cleanup, and doctor.
apply.detail: The shell stays Bash 3.2-compatible for stock macOS.

verify.name: Internet + Report
verify.desc: Update supported internet-downloaded apps only when installed, then write inventory and run history.
verify.detail: Coverage reports separate supported installed, supported missing, and unknown apps.

showcase.title: Operational surfaces, not mystery dialogs.
showcase.subtitle: Use the CLI for runs, coverage reports for gaps, and dev_sync for private machine state.

captions.overview: Build APPLICATIONS.md from this Mac before updating.
captions.categories: See supported installed apps, supported missing apps, and unknown installed apps.
captions.runCenter: Run all layers or skip specific layers with CLI flags.
captions.apps: Keep personal inventory out of the public repository.
captions.aiTools: Scaffold handlers for new internet apps.
captions.insights: Append update history to UPDATES.md after each run.
captions.help: Docs cover users, operators, agents, and private overlay setup.

features.title: Built for Macs that need
features.subtitle: A local inventory, strict update order, seven languages, and handlers for the update paths Apple and vendors actually use.

pipeline.title: Seven-step pipeline
pipeline.desc: Prescan → system → App Store → CLI/npm → Homebrew → internet apps → postupdate.

snapshot.title: Coverage reporting
snapshot.desc: Report supported installed apps, supported missing apps, unknown apps, and method categories.

frontends.title: Inventory-first install
frontends.desc: install.sh builds APPLICATIONS.md from this Mac and updates only already-installed software.

scheduler.title: Critical safety rules
scheduler.desc: softwareupdate always keeps -R; App Store updates use sudo mas upgrade.

aiTools.title: Agent-friendly handlers
aiTools.desc: Scaffold and test new internet app handlers through the public registry.

suggestionsAi.title: Private dev_sync overlay
suggestionsAi.desc: Sync inventory, update history, preferences, and secrets outside the public Git tree.

plugins.title: Run tests and secret scans
plugins.desc: run_tests.sh checks syntax, registry parity, safety rules, and secret exposure.

multihost.title: Seven-language UI
multihost.desc: English, Polish, German, French, Spanish, Italian, and Portuguese shell messages.

editions.title: Apple Silicon focus
editions.desc: Designed for arm64 Macs running macOS 13 or newer.

vanillaJs.title: Bash 3.2 compatible
vanillaJs.desc: No associative arrays, mapfile, or modern Bash assumptions.

platformSupport.title: Covers the update paths your Mac actually uses.
platformSupport.subtitle: macOS Updates coordinates Apple, Homebrew, npm, native CLI, Microsoft, Google, GitHub-release, Docker, Sparkle, and built-in vendor updaters.
platformSupport.note: Internet apps are only updated when already installed. Missing supported apps are reported; unknown installed apps are listed for handler work.

macos.name: Apple Silicon macOS
macos.status: Production-ready · macOS 13+ · arm64 only · local inventory · 40+ internet apps · 7 languages

managerDetail.appstore: App Store updates through sudo mas upgrade plus AppleScript fallback for iPad apps.
managerDetail.cli-npm: Node, Bun, npm globals, and developer AI CLIs.
managerDetail.internet: Keystone, GitHub DMG, msupdate, Docker CLI, silent-launch, Sparkle, and manual handlers.
managerDetail.dev-sync: Optional private overlay for machine-local files.

howItWorks.title: Install, preview, update.
howItWorks.subtitle: The workflow is direct: build local inventory, dry-run the orchestrator, then run the full update when ready.

step1.label: Install
step1.title: Build this Mac's inventory
step1.desc: The installer clones the repo, configures dependencies, asks for language, and builds APPLICATIONS.md from installed software.

step2.label: Preview
step2.title: Run without applying changes
step2.desc: Use update_all.sh --dry-run -y or the coverage report to inspect what the updater can handle.

step3.label: Update
step3.title: Run all layers in order
step3.desc: update_all.sh orchestrates every supported update category and records postupdate state.

openSource.title: Public code. Private machine state.
openSource.subtitle: MIT licensed scripts and docs live in GitHub. Personal inventory, update history, preferences, and secrets stay local or in your private overlay.
openSource.builtInOpenDesc: The repo keeps update methods, dispatch order, docs, i18n strings, and tests visible. You can inspect every handler before running it.

installation.title: Start with the one-line installer.
installation.subtitle: New users get setup plus a fresh inventory. Owners can restore private overlay afterward.

seo.homeTitle: macOS Updates — One-command updater for Apple Silicon Macs
seo.homeDesc: Production-ready updater for macOS, App Store, Homebrew, CLI/npm tools, and 40+ internet apps. Local inventory first.
seo.featuresTitle: macOS Updates features — Update coverage and local inventory
seo.featuresDesc: Explore the seven-step update pipeline, coverage reporting, private overlay, and Apple Silicon safety rules.
seo.docsTitle: macOS Updates docs — Install, operate, and extend
seo.docsDesc: Install macOS Updates, run dry-runs, understand the update pipeline, and add internet app handlers.
seo.securityDesc: How macOS Updates protects local inventory, private overlay files, and privileged update operations.
seo.changelogDesc: Release history for the Apple Silicon macOS update orchestrator.
seo.contributingDesc: Add internet app handlers, improve docs, and keep Bash 3.2-compatible update scripts tested.

roadmap.title: Coverage keeps expanding
roadmap.subtitle: The public registry and handler tests make new app support explicit.

signing.title: More handlers
signing.desc: Add verified support for more internet-downloaded apps.

installers.title: Better run UX
installers.desc: Sharper terminal summaries for long update sessions.

marketplace.title: Handler recipes
marketplace.desc: Document common patterns for GitHub DMG, Sparkle, Keystone, and silent-launch apps.

i18nMore.title: Locale polish
i18nMore.desc: Keep all seven language files aligned with new features.

telemetry.title: No telemetry
telemetry.desc: Improve local reports without sending usage data anywhere.

parallel.title: Safer automation
parallel.desc: Make scheduled or agent-driven runs easier to audit.

q1.q: Is macOS Updates free and open source?
q1.a: Yes. The public code is MIT licensed and runs locally. Machine-specific files are intentionally gitignored.

q2.q: Will it install apps I do not already have?
q2.a: No. The updater only updates software already installed on the Mac. Supported missing apps are reported, not installed.

q3.a: The project is scoped to arm64 Macs and Homebrew at /opt/homebrew. Intel Macs are intentionally unsupported.

q4.a: Yes. Run bash update_all.sh --dry-run -y or bash scripts/report_update_coverage.sh to inspect coverage first.

q5.a: dev_sync handles optional private overlay files such as APPLICATIONS.md, UPDATES.md, .env, preferences, and cloud sync configuration.

q6.q: Why does mas upgrade use sudo?
q6.a: The project follows the current macOS/App Store CLI requirement documented in the repo, including the CVE-related Sequoia behavior.

q7.a: The current registry covers 40+ internet-downloaded apps plus macOS, App Store, Homebrew, and CLI/npm tooling.

q8.q: How do I add a new internet app?
q8.a: Use scripts/scaffold_internet_app.sh, register the method and dispatch order, add i18n strings, then run bash run_tests.sh.
